query: escaping vs sanitizing xss interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this browser, network, security, or state-management prompt, so broader JavaScript question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
competitors.selectionReason: OWASP is the strongest canonical security source for XSS prevention, output encoding, and defense-in-depth guidance.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative security reference for context-specific encoding and XSS prevention rules.
theirGaps: It is broader and more defensive than the narrow interview contrast between escaping and sanitizing as two different tools.

realWorldUseCases: Explain the difference between output escaping (context-specific encoding) and sanitizing user input (allowlist filtering). Include when to use each, how they work, and why both matter for XSS prevention.
actionableExamples: Real-world scenario A profile bio can be plain text (escape and render safely), but a blog editor might allow limited rich text (sanitize with an allowlist). Decide based on the product requirement, not convenience.
followUpConfusion: Common pitfalls - Stripping tags with regex (easy to bypass). - Sanitizing once and reusing the result in a different context. - Double-escaping by encoding the same value twice. - Storing only sanitized HTML and losing the original data.

realWorldUseCases: OWASP is stronger on the full defense-in-depth model, but our page is more direct for the interview prompt because it contrasts escaping and sanitizing through one product decision.
actionableExamples: The cheat sheet still has strong rule-based examples, so the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the likely follow-up traps: regex stripping, double-escaping, context reuse, and losing original raw content.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny attribute-context escaping example later if we need more example distance over OWASP.

competitors.winReason: OWASP is the canonical XSS defense reference, but our page is stronger for this interview question because it isolates the escape-versus-sanitize decision in a simpler, product-facing answer.

competitors.label: MDN XSS
competitors.selectionReason: MDN has a direct XSS guide and is a strong practical comparator for escaping, sanitization, and trusted rendering behavior.
theirStrengths: It is practical on how XSS happens and how browser rendering contexts change safety rules.
theirGaps: It is less compressed into the interview-sized distinction between escaping text and sanitizing allowed markup.

realWorldUseCases: Explain the difference between output escaping (context-specific encoding) and sanitizing user input (allowlist filtering). Include when to use each, how they work, and why both matter for XSS prevention.
actionableExamples: Real-world scenario A profile bio can be plain text (escape and render safely), but a blog editor might allow limited rich text (sanitize with an allowlist). Decide based on the product requirement, not convenience.
followUpConfusion: Common pitfalls - Stripping tags with regex (easy to bypass). - Sanitizing once and reusing the result in a different context. - Double-escaping by encoding the same value twice. - Storing only sanitized HTML and losing the original data.

realWorldUseCases: MDN is practical on XSS defense, but our page is more explicit about the product-level decision between plain text and limited rich text.
actionableExamples: Its guidance is still practical and example-driven, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the next confusion interviewers ask about: why sanitizing HTML does not replace context-specific output encoding.

actionableExamples: Keep the real-world scenario and pitfalls adjacent so the answer stays more decision-oriented than a broader XSS overview.

competitors.winReason: MDN is practical on XSS defense, but our page is stronger because it frames escaping and sanitizing as two different tools chosen by content requirements.

competitors.label: DOMPurify
competitors.selectionReason: DOMPurify is a direct sanitization reference and a strong comparator for the allowlist-markup side of this interview question.
theirStrengths: It is topic-direct on safe HTML sanitization and practical allowlist-based markup cleaning.
theirGaps: It only covers one side of the interview prompt and does not replace context-specific escaping.

realWorldUseCases: Explain the difference between output escaping (context-specific encoding) and sanitizing user input (allowlist filtering). Include when to use each, how they work, and why both matter for XSS prevention.
actionableExamples: Real-world scenario A profile bio can be plain text (escape and render safely), but a blog editor might allow limited rich text (sanitize with an allowlist). Decide based on the product requirement, not convenience.
followUpConfusion: Common pitfalls - Stripping tags with regex (easy to bypass). - Sanitizing once and reusing the result in a different context. - Double-escaping by encoding the same value twice. - Storing only sanitized HTML and losing the original data.

realWorldUseCases: DOMPurify is strong on sanitization itself, but our page is more complete for the actual prompt because it contrasts sanitization with escaping instead of treating sanitization as the whole answer.
actionableExamples: It is still practical and relevant on the allowlist side of the problem, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the likely follow-up confusion that sanitization and escaping solve different threats and must not be swapped blindly.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one short attribute-encoding example to make the escaping side even more concrete next to the sanitization scenario.

competitors.winReason: DOMPurify is strong on sanitization, but our page is stronger for this prompt because it explains when sanitization is appropriate and when escaping is still required.
